[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Mckay-hall-parkville.jpg/250px-Mckay-hall-parkville.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mckay-hall-parkville.jpg)

Mackay Hall

**Park University** is a [private university](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") in [Parkville, Missouri](/wiki/Parkville,_Missouri "Parkville, Missouri"), United States. It was founded in 1875.[[4]](#cite_note-NCES-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)
In the fall of 2025, Park had an enrollment of 4,949 students.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Park_University&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The school which was originally called **Park College** was founded in 1875[[7]](#cite_note-7) by John A. McAfee on land donated by [George S. Park](/wiki/George_S._Park "George S. Park") with its initial structure being the stone hotel Park owned on the bluff above the Missouri River.

The original concept called for students to receive free tuition and board in exchange for working up to half day in the college's farm, electrical shop or printing plant. According to the terms of the arrangement if the "Parkville Experiment" did not work out within five years, the college grounds were to revert to Park.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

There were 17 students in the first school year and in the first graduation class there were five women. McAfee led until his death in 1890. His son Lowell M. McAfee became the second president of Park until stepping down in 1913. The first international student at Park University arrived in 1880 from Japan.

The defining landmark of the campus is [Mackay Hall](/wiki/Mackay_Hall "Mackay Hall"), named after [Carroll County, Illinois](/wiki/Carroll_County,_Illinois "Carroll County, Illinois") banker Duncan Mackay who donated $25,000 in materials for the structure shortly before his death.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The building was constructed using [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") mined on the campus grounds and built with the labor of students. Construction began in 1883 and was finished by 1893. Mackay Hall is the main focal point of the campus and dominates the hillside, overlooking the town of Parkville. It is on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").

For decades the school was affiliated with the Presbyterian Church but it no longer has that affiliation.[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] The college has had a relationship with the military since 1889. However, the relationship was greatly expanded in the late 1960s with the establishment of a Military Degree Completion Program and later in 1972 with the Military Resident Center System. Park's total enrollment has grown from its small base since 1996 when it first began offering online courses.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 2000, it was renamed **Park University**.

## Locations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Park_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Locations")]

### Parkville campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Park_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Parkville campus")]

The flagship campus of Park University is in the city of [Parkville, Missouri](/wiki/Parkville,_Missouri "Parkville, Missouri"). The Park University Graduate School is in downtown [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"). There are 41 campuses in 22 [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state")[[11]](#cite_note-11) including four campuses in the Kansas City area (downtown Kansas City, [Independence](/wiki/Independence,_Missouri "Independence, Missouri"), [Lenexa](/wiki/Lenexa,_Kansas "Lenexa, Kansas"), and Parkville) and a campus center in [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"). Most of the [satellite campuses](/wiki/Satellite_campus "Satellite campus") are on or near United States military bases and share quarters with other businesses/organizations.

### Gilbert campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Park_University&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Gilbert campus")]

In 2018, the university opened a campus center in [Gilbert, Arizona](/wiki/Gilbert,_Arizona "Gilbert, Arizona"), in the city's Heritage District. Park leased 11,000 square feet (1,000 m2) at the University Building.[[12]](#cite_note-ParkGilbert2019-12) The university continued to expand the Gilbert campus in 2019, leasing an additional 7,000 square feet (650 m2) and extending the initial three-year term with the city to five years.[[12]](#cite_note-ParkGilbert2019-12) The university continued to expand the Gilbert campus with the addition of college athletics.[[12]](#cite_note-ParkGilbert2019-12) Initially playing as an independent during the 2019–20 academic year, the university announced membership in the NAIA and [California Pacific Conference](/wiki/California_Pacific_Conference "California Pacific Conference") (Pac West) starting in 2020–21.[[13]](#cite_note-2020NAIAnewmembers-13)

## Rankings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Park_University&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rankings")]

* Ranked second in the “online and nontraditional” category on the *Military Times’* "Best for Vets: Colleges 2016" list.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* Ranked second among all private colleges/universities in the country with a 9.5 percent annual ROI by 2015 PayScale College ROI Report.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* 2015-16 Colleges of Distinction list.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* Ranked by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* as the 126-165 best Midwest college in 2018.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Park_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Athletics")]

### Park Pirates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Park_University&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Park Pirates")]

The athletic teams of the Park University main campus are called the Pirates. The university is a member of the [National Association of Intercollegiate Athletics](/wiki/National_Association_of_Intercollegiate_Athletics "National Association of Intercollegiate Athletics") (NAIA), primarily competing in the [Heart of America Athletic Conference](/wiki/Heart_of_America_Athletic_Conference "Heart of America Athletic Conference") (HAAC) since the 2020–21 academic year. The Pirates previously competed in the [American Midwest Conference](/wiki/American_Midwest_Conference "American Midwest Conference") (AMC) from 2009–10 to 2019–20; which they were a member on a previous stint from 1986–87 to 1993–94; and in the defunct [Midlands Collegiate Athletic Conference](/wiki/Midlands_Collegiate_Athletic_Conference "Midlands Collegiate Athletic Conference") (MCAC) from 1994–95 to 2008–09.

Park competes in 18 intercollegiate varsity sports: Men's sports include baseball, basketball, cross country, golf, soccer, track & field (indoor and outdoor) and volleyball; while women's sports include basketball, cross country, golf, soccer, softball, track & field (indoor and outdoor) and volleyball; and co-ed sports include eSports.

The Department of Athletics at Park University was led by [Claude English](/wiki/Claude_English "Claude English"), Director of Athletics, who was also the Pirates’ men's basketball coach from 1992 to 2005 and retired in 2021. From 1980 to 1984, English was the head men's basketball coach at his alma mater, the [University of Rhode Island](/wiki/University_of_Rhode_Island "University of Rhode Island"), and he played one season in the NBA with the [Portland Trail Blazers](/wiki/Portland_Trail_Blazers "Portland Trail Blazers") in 1970–1971.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Seven former Park Pirates competed for the [Kansas City Comets](/wiki/Kansas_City_Comets_(2010%E2%80%93) "Kansas City Comets (2010–)") of the [Major Arena Soccer League](/wiki/Major_Arena_Soccer_League "Major Arena Soccer League") as of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Park_University&action=edit).[[18]](#cite_note-18)

#### Championships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Park_University&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Championships")]

* 2018 Women's Volleyball (NAIA National Champions)
* 2017 Men's Volleyball (NAIA Invitational Tournament)
* 2014 Women's Volleyball (NAIA National Champions)
* 2014 Men's Volleyball (NAIA Invitational Tournament)
* 2012 Men's Volleyball (NAIA Invitational Tournament)
* 2008 Men's Volleyball (NAIA Invitational Tournament)
* 2003 Men's Volleyball (NAIA Invitational Tournament)

### Park–Gilbert Buccaneers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Park_University&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Park–Gilbert Buccaneers")]

The athletic teams of the Gilbert campus of Park University (Park–Gilbert) are called the Buccaneers.[[12]](#cite_note-ParkGilbert2019-12) The university added a college athletics program to the Gilbert campus center in 2019.[[12]](#cite_note-ParkGilbert2019-12) After playing as independent institution during the 2019–20 academic year, the university announced membership in the [National Association of Intercollegiate Athletics](/wiki/National_Association_of_Intercollegiate_Athletics "National Association of Intercollegiate Athletics") (NAIA), primarily competing in the [California Pacific Conference](/wiki/California_Pacific_Conference "California Pacific Conference") (Cal Pac) starting in the 2020–21 academic year.[[13]](#cite_note-2020NAIAnewmembers-13)

Park–Gilbert competes in 15 intercollegiate varsity sports: Men's sports include baseball, basketball, cross country, golf, soccer, track & field (indoor and outdoor) and volleyball; while women's sports include basketball, beach volleyball, cross country, golf, soccer, softball, track & field (indoor and outdoor) and volleyball.

* [Vlatko Andonovski](/wiki/Vlatko_Andonovski "Vlatko Andonovski") (2008) - professional soccer coach

(1969) - former [New Jersey General Assemblyman](/wiki/New_Jersey_General_Assembly "New Jersey General Assembly") and [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") Director of [Consumer Affairs](/wiki/Consumer_Protection "Consumer Protection")

### Faculty and staff

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Park_University&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Faculty and staff")]

* [Stanislav Ioudenitch](/wiki/Stanislav_Ioudenitch "Stanislav Ioudenitch") (Professor of Music and Piano) - Pianist and gold medalist of [Van Cliburn International Piano Competition](/wiki/Van_Cliburn_International_Piano_Competition "Van Cliburn International Piano Competition") in 2001
* [Kay Barnes](/wiki/Kay_Barnes "Kay Barnes") (Senior Director for University Engagement) - Former mayor of [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri") and candidate for Congress in 2008[[22]](#cite_note-22)
